coredata.dc:description: With the increase in global population, environmental hazards, climate change, energy limitations, and a decrease in agricultural land, emerging techniques are critical to raising and enhancing food production and quality. This review aims to draw attention to the latest developments in technology that guarantee the quality and safety of food. It is devoted to highlighting several key materials-based derivatives, extraction methods, chemistry, mechanisms of action, safety considerations, and various other uses. The review discusses the utilization of cutting-edge food materials, including aerogels, C-dots, nanoparticles, nanoemulsions, and conjugates, in several areas of food processing and preservation. These materials offer unique properties that contribute to improving food quality, safety, and shelf life. The review also addresses research challenges and identifies future trends in this field. These challenges may involve optimising synthesis processes, understanding the long-term effects of these materials on human health and the environment, and developing sustainable production methods. By addressing these challenges, researchers can unlock the full potential of advanced food materials. In conclusion, this review underscores the significance of emerging techniques and materials in ensuring food quality and safety. The integration of science and engineering in the development of advanced food materials opens up new possibilities for sustainable food production and preservation. By addressing current challenges and focusing on future trends, researchers can contribute to the advancement of this field and meet growing global food demands.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the increase in global population, environmental hazards, climate change, energy limitations, and a decrease in agricultural land, emerging techniques are critical to raising and enhancing food production and quality. This review aims to draw attention to the latest developments in technology that guarantee the quality and safety of food. It is devoted to highlighting several key materials-based derivatives, extraction methods, chemistry, mechanisms of action, safety considerations, and various other uses. The review discusses the utilization of cutting-edge food materials, including aerogels, C-dots, nanoparticles, nanoemulsions, and conjugates, in several areas of food processing and preservation. These materials offer unique properties that contribute to improving food quality, safety, and shelf life. The review also addresses research challenges and identifies future trends in this field. These challenges may involve optimising synthesis processes, understanding the long-term effects of these materials on human health and the environment, and developing sustainable production methods. By addressing these challenges, researchers can unlock the full potential of advanced food materials. In conclusion, this review underscores the significance of emerging techniques and materials in ensuring food quality and safety. The integration of science and engineering in the development of advanced food materials opens up new possibilities for sustainable food production and preservation. By addressing current challenges and focusing on future trends, researchers can contribute to the advancement of this field and meet growing global food demands.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: By 2050, it's expected that there will be 9.7 billion people on the planet. It will be more challenging to guarantee that everyone has access to wholesome, nutritious food as the world's population rises. There is a high demand for specialty crops, including fruits, nuts, and vegetables, as well as meat and dairy products, because of rising incomes in emerging economies and better living circumstances (Food and Agriculture Organization, 2017). Furthermore, an increase in agricultural productivity of at least 50% is required to meet the world's food demand by the year 2025. Consumers in rich countries, like those in underdeveloped countries, have developed demands for niche items promoted as organic, fair-trade, or locally grown (Bellows et al., 2010). Soil erosion and the loss of biodiverse habitats are two effects of the increased food demand that are putting a burden on natural resources (Feldmann & Hamm, 2015; O’Connor & Kelly, 2017). Food safety and sustainable food production are therefore becoming increasingly important issues (Tilman et al., 2011; Cheng et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2023).

Recent advances in food engineering and technology have transformed several scientific and industrial areas, including the food industry. Emerging food materials integrate interdisciplinary scientific knowledge into a single domain within food materials science and engineering. It applies the principles of other disciplines, such as chemical sciences, polymer science, and omics, to food science. Applications of advanced food materials like aerogels, nanoparticles, nanoemulsions, conjugates, modified biopolymers, and modified genes and microbes have become increasingly important to be used in a variety of fields relating to food science and technology (Cheng et al., 2022; Fang et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2023; Sharma et al., 2023). These novel materials typically serve as a component of the food item and aid in the prevention of degradation, hence extending the shelf life of the food material containing them. Fig. 1 a and b indicate the recently published literature about such engineering materials. Pertaining to this, there is a huge opportunity to improve food safety and increase agricultural productivity in sustainable terms through the development of advanced technologies and industry (Xiao & Mujumdar, 2020; Eliasson, 2015; Lesk et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2021; 2022). The interrelationship between food ingredients, food security and safety, and food processing is represented in Fig. 1c. The present review seeks to explicate numerous essential, specifically developed engineered materials based on several important factors such as derivatives, extraction, chemistry, modes of action, ensuring safety, and several other uses. Nonetheless, the majority of functional materials have been described and examined in the literature; additional in-depth and complete understanding of the chosen materials, such as C-dots, nanoparticles, aerogels, and conjugates, is necessary. The toxicity and safety of these carefully developed materials will also be extensively reviewed in order for them to be used safely in food items and/or formulations. The various sections of the review based on various chosen functional materials will be a study of those elements that deserve attention in many areas and must be put into our food to maintain its quality, safety, and shelf stability.

Aerogels have emerged as important materials in several sectors due to the desirable characteristics they provide. They are developed by removing pore fluid from a gel that have extremely particular physical features such as low bulk density, high porosity (mostly in the mesoporous region), and high specific surface area (≥150 m2/g). Due to their distinctive insulating qualities and high loading capabilities, these materials are used in a variety of industries, such as food fortification and functional packaging (Dhua et al., 2022; Dhua & Mishra, 2023; Manzocco et al., 2021). The use of aerogels in the food industry considering their formulation, synthesis process, and main functionalities are discussed in the later section.

It is well known that the food packaging industry must evolve towards active and intelligent packaging that is not only environmentally friendly in its production and disposal but also preserves food and even extends its shelf-life. Each form of packaging structure is needed to satisfy certain standards based on its purpose. One of these packaging structures is absorbent pads (Gaikwad et al., 2019). These pads are typically used in packaging to reduce the excess liquid exuded by fresh products during storage, which is a key factor in maintaining and increasing shelf-life. The lightweight and porous structure of aerogels makes them ideal candidates for this purpose (Benito- González et al., 2021; Benito-González et al., 2020; de Oliveira, Bruni, Fabra, et al., 2019a,b).

The top and bottom layers of these pads are typically made of a non-permeable/non-adherent synthetic polymer, such as polyethylene, and a hydrophilic non-woven interlayer (Benito-González et al., 2020, 2021; de Oliveira, Bruni, Fabra, et al., 2019a,b). Active substances, which have been proven to be effective against bacteria growth, are usually present in this transitional layer (Fig. 2 ). The food packaging industry is aiming to employ renewable and sustainable resources for the formulation of its packaging systems due to the environmental issues connected with the usage of petroleum-derived goods (Benito-González et al., 2020; Fontes-Candia et al., 2019). Studies conducted in recent years show promising results in the use of bio-based and highly available polymers for the preparation of aerogels applied as absorbent pads (Budtova et al., 2020; Fontes-Candia et al., 2019).

In the manufacture of aerogels, before the final drying step, the parameters to be considered are those involved in the hydrogel formulation. Precursor material, functional group precursor material, pH, and cross-linker concentration must all be carefully considered in order to produce high-performance aerogels. Regarding the precursor material, several bio-based polymers as well as biodegradable synthetic polymers (i.e., PVA) are being used as substitutes for petroleum-derived plastics (Budtova et al., 2020; Fontes-Candia et al., 2019). The most widely used bio-based polymers for use as aerogel matrix include starch and various hemicelluloses, as well as cellulose and its derivatives such as cellulose nanofibers (CNF), cellulose nanocrystals (CNC), bacterial cellulose (BC), and microcrystalline cellulose (MCC). The addition of a cross-linker will depend on whether the type of crosslinking to be achieved in the matrix is chemical in nature. These cross-linkers are more common when the main hydrogel matrix is a blending between polymers. In addition, when dealing with food contact materials, the choice and concentration of the cross-linker are important. The most common cross-linkers in aerogels used as absorbent pads are glutaraldehyde, calcium chloride, and citric acid (Pan et al., 2021; Udoetok et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2022).

Regarding the drying method for the aerogel, there are several techniques, the most common being freeze-drying and supercritical CO2. In the case of freeze-drying, it is a two-step procedure: (i) freezing of the wet gel material (range −45 to −15 °C) to obtain the solid state and (ii) sublimation that converts the liquid into gas under vacuum. This second stage is where the pores of the aerogel will be formed (Benito- González et al., 2020; Benito-González et al., 2021; de Oliveira, Bruni, Fabra, et al., 2019a,b). A very interesting parameter to control in this technique is the freezing direction, since it is directly related to the distribution, type, and shape of pores in the final aerogel (Ni et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2021). Supercritical fluids, on the other hand, are used more often because of the liquid and gas qualities they provide, without any gas-liquid interface or surface tension, enabling modification of solvent strength and density. Due to its affordability, abundant availability, lack of toxicity, chemical inertness, and mellow working conditions, CO2 is one of the most often employed supercritical fluids (Dhua et al., 2022; Dhua & Mishra, 2023).

There are many characteristics specific to the structure of aerogels, but when applied as absorbent pads the most important parameters to consider are those interlinked to the ability to absorb exudates and, in active packaging, to absorb and release active compounds (Dhua et al., 2022; Dhua & Mishra, 2023; He et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2022).

As a result, the porosity will be inversely proportional to the density of aerogels and will rely directly on the concentration of the matrix precursor (Zaman et al., 2020). The porosity of aerogels is often more than 95%, and it only marginally decreases when the network contains significant quantities of fiber (Yang et al., 2022; He et al., 2022). The most creative research on the creation of bio-based aerogels for use as absorbent pads in food items are included in Table 1 .

Water absorption capacity (WAC) is directly proportional to the absorbency of the pads. By establishing a balance between water absorption and water retention forces, it is possible to obtain the utmost WAC of the material. Low density and high specific surface area are conducive to high WAC values, which are also attributable to the hydrophilic nature of matrix precursors (Yang et al., 2022; He et al., 2022). In fresh food packaging applications, a high WAC property value is desired because it reduces moisture condensation, thereby protecting the food from bacterial activity-induced deterioration (de Oliveira et al., 2019a). Finally, the effective performance of these pads in food products should not be limited to excellent absorption behavior, but should also exhibit bioactive properties, such as antioxidant and antimicrobial capacity (de Oliveira, Bruni, Fabra, et al., 2019a,b, 2020). These bioactive characteristics have been achieved thanks to the incorporation of natural extracts in the aerogel, such as extracts of Posidonia oceanica, Gelidium sesquipedale, Arundo donax, or yerba mate, active compounds such as thymol or even silver nanoparticles (Benito-González et al., 2021; Fontes-Candia et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2022). The release rate of these compounds is a key parameter to control their action on the food, recommending a slow and gradual release to achieve a longer shelf-life.

The possibilities offered by aerogel structures when functionalized with natural extracts or bioactive compounds have already been mentioned, as the sustained and prolonged release of these compounds opens up a new approach for packaging actives. However, this property has also been exploited in the food industry by employing aerogels as delivery systems for active substances beneficial to human health (Dogenski et al., 2020; Fonseca et al., 2021; Pan et al., 2021; Parikka et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2022).

The main advantage of aerogels over other structures is that they can be impregnated at any stage of their preparation. For this purpose, several strategies have been studied, the two main ones being wet impregnation, if the target molecule is impregnated at any stage of hydrogel formation, and post-drying impregnation once the final aerogel has been obtained (Dhua et al., 2022; Dhua & Mishra, 2023). During wet impregnation, the target molecule is either directly incorporated and dissolved in any of the solvents involved in the aerogel formulation or is vehiculated with supercritical fluids during drying. In these cases, the efficiency of the impregnation will depend on the affinity of the target molecule for the phases that make up the hydrogel. This approach is more effective when phenolic compounds are used instead of oils (Manzocco et al., 2021). Simply submerging the aerogel in a liquid that contains the target molecule will do this in the event of post-drying impregnation. However, supercritical CO2 as an auxiliary solvent is the most common technique for post-drying impregnation. This method results in higher impregnation efficiency for non-polar substances like oil (Manzocco et al., 2021). Regarding this, a large body of research has been done employing aerogels as delivery methods for a controlled release of vitamins, medications, and nutraceuticals that contain the ideal therapeutic dosage and improve the bioavailability of the same substances. Micronutrient fortification is an application area with the goal of improving the nutritional profile and quality of the food (Selvasekaran & Chidambaram, 2022). Table 2 provides a summary of the latest publications in this area.

Aerogels derived from natural polysaccharides and proteins have the advantage of being sustainable, renewable, and edible, making them ideal for micronutrient fortification (Selvasekaran & Chidambaram, 2021). The internal structure of aerogels and the sort of reactivity they have with micronutrients are related to their loading capacity. Therefore, while using aerogels for this purpose, it's crucial to manage the pore size and specific surface area. The impregnated aerogels must also maintain their stability throughout storage and prevent micronutrient leakage. It is important to look into these materials' storage, shelf-life, and digestibility further (Selvasekaran & Chidambaram, 2022).

The main advantage of aerogels over other structures is that they can be impregnated at any stage of their preparation. Aerogels made from natural polysaccharides and proteins are sustainable, renewable, and edible, which makes them perfect for adding micronutrients (Selvasekaran & Chidambaram, 2021).

Regarding aerogels, there are some uncharted study territory in this area that has to be investigated regarding their use in the food sector. One thing about the usage of aerogels in the food business is that EU food rules have not yet addressed it. This means that everything related to the aerogel's production process must be considered, as well as the final aerogels' safety with regard to volatile residues. Furthermore, since it calls for highly pure fractions, the manufacture of bio-based aerogels is quite costly (Santos et al., 2020; Pan et al., 2021; He et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2022).

In order to precisely determine the nature and kinetics of the interaction of aerogels with biological tissues, new methodologies must be developed. These problems are anticipated to be resolved in the next years, making aerogels a viable and long-lasting option for the food sector (Dhua et al., 2022; Dhua & Mishra, 2023).

Due to the distinctive features of nanoscale emulsions, such as their high stability, appealing look, excellent performance, and sensory benefit, interest in them has significantly increased in recent years. One of the most widely used formulation technologies in the pharmaceutical and cosmeceutical industries is nanoemulsions (Gutierrez et al., 2008). Nanoemulsions have developed quickly as a method for delivering bioactive chemicals and medications in cosmetic and dermatological formulations due to their high kinetic stability, small droplet size, and thermodynamic stability. The composition and method of preparation play a significant role in determining the quality of nanoemulsions (Sonneville-Aubrun et al., 2004). They are primarily focused on high performance, consumer product distribution, and potential for mass production. Since they can provide improved material characteristics and adaptability, nanoemulsions are replacing microscale emulsions and are being used in more and more applications. Nanoemulsions with metastability are dispersions of sub-100 nm droplets of one liquid in another immiscible liquid. Several techniques, particularly high-flow emulsification, have been used for decades to generate these materials. Recently advanced nanoemulsions are being created as a result of advancements in formulation, emulsification, and characterization that have desirable functionality and well-controlled physical features (Jafari, He, & Bhandari, 2007a, 2007b). Although nanoemulsions share several characteristics with their bigger micron and sub-micron emulsion counterparts, several significant distinctions become apparent as the droplet size is lowered to the regime of nanoscale (Fryd & Mason, 2012).

Long-lived, metastable nanoemulsions can be calibrated and regulated for their mechanical, optical, and transport properties while simultaneously including other desirable components like coatings, biomolecules, and chemical additives. Overall, these developments offer guidelines that enable designers to create nanoemulsions with more desirable compositions and behaviors (Hatanaka et al., 2010; Silva et al., 2012). The fundamental ideas of emulsions also apply to nanoemulsions. Over the past ten years, several evaluations that particularly discuss nanoemulsion systems have been published, in addition to those that deal with microscale and larger emulsions (Gutierrez et al., 2008). The generation and stability of nanoemulsions have been discussed in some detail, with an emphasis on the potential functions of surfactants and the coarsening of the droplet size distribution. Additionally, topics including low-energy emulsification (Izquierdo et al., 2002), ideal emulsification settings (Jafari et al., 2007a, 2007b), and skincare applications have been explored (Sonneville-Aubrun et al., 2004). Metastable nanoemulsions are distinct from lyotropic microemulsion phases, which are thermodynamically stable microemulsions (Melson et al., 2004). While swollen micellar microemulsions and nanoemulsions can resemble one another, microemulsions arise by a process known as thermodynamic self-assembly, and as a result, they are a different kind of physical system (Becher, 1996; Melson et al., 2004).

In contrast, most microemulsion phases have highly relative solubilized molecular components that spontaneously self-assemble into a variety of nanostructures with sizes and morphologies determined by composition and temperature (Langevin, 1992; Whitesides and Grzybowski, 2002). As a result, equilibrium phase diagrams can include microemulsion phases; nevertheless, according to the term's most widely accepted meaning, no equilibrium phase diagram should ever include nanoemulsion phases.

When examining droplet size, shape, volume fraction, and interaction in nano-emulsions, optical, X-ray, and neutron scattering are useful approaches. These approaches are used for a variety of dispersed materials, including nano-emulsions, however the interpretation sometimes needs a model and may not be novel (Fryd & Mason, 2012; Salvia-Trujillo et al., 2019). Optical scattering methods include diffusing-wave spectroscopy, refractive index measurements, static and dynamic light scattering, and more. Dynamic light scattering often gives the mean hydrodynamic radius of nanodroplets diffusing at extremely low concentrations. Diffusing-wave spectroscopy helps us comprehend dynamic light scattering beyond the single scattering limit (Salvia-Trujillo et al., 2019).

Static light scattering can detect droplet sizes at microscale and perhaps nanoemulsions. SAXS can also measure the wavenumber-dependent intensity I(q) of silicone O/W nanoemulsions without contrast enhancers. X-ray photon correlation spectroscopy can analyse droplet dynamics (Guzey & McClements & Rao, 2011; Salvia-Trujillo et al., 2019). Selective molecule deuteration can influence neutron scattering contrast, similar to how nanoemulsion component variations in neutron scattering length density may provide enough contrast for SANS data collection. Since nanoemulsions have very small droplet sizes, optical microscopies are typically particularly helpful for characterizing microscale emulsions. However, some microscopy techniques do offer a spatial resolution that permits direct observation of nanoscopic objects (Fryd & Mason, 2012).

Microscopy techniques such as negative-staining TEM, freeze-fracture TEM, and C-TEM, have been used to obtain real-space pictures of nanoemulsions (Goindi et al., 2016; Silva et al., 2012). The images obtained from these techniques demonstrate internal morphologies and surface structure of nanoemulsions. While Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), and X-ray diffraction (XRD) are used to investigate the physicochemical properties of materials, differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) is used to determine phase transitions (melting of crystalline areas and the number of ice crystals in nanoemulsions) (Goindi et al., 2016; Silva et al., 2012).

The bioactive components of food are degraded during food preparation whiles others are very volatile and affected by processing conditions (Aswathanarayan & Vittal, 2019). Whereas some others have poor solubility but have fast metabolism thereby reducing their bioavailability. These problems can be solved by encapsulating bioactive compounds in the food matrix (Abdou et al., 2018). In addition to protecting from processing conditions due to light, pH, temperature, and oxidation observed over storage, a bioactive substance is protected through encapsulation which prevents its degradation as time goes by. Nanoemulsions are an efficient way to improve food's digestibility and regulate how quickly bioactive substances are released into the body and absorbed there. Additionally, improvements are made to the pharmacological activity of some compounds, the solubility of medications, and the bioavailability of active substances are improved by nanoemulsions (Fryd & Mason, 2012). Nutraceuticals and antioxidants, as well as other valuable compounds and active ingredients, can be encapsulated in nanoemulsions. They are advantageous for the regulated release of flavorings in food as well (Table 3 ).

For potential uses in food packaging, nanoemulsions can be added to coatings and films. To increase the shelf-life of foods such as dairy products, meats, fruits, and vegetables, edible nanocoating based on nanoemulsions that contain flavourings, colourings, enzymes, antioxidants, and antimicrobials can be applied (de Oliveira Filho, Miranda, Ferreira, & Plotto, 2021). Additionally, coatings can reduce moisture, gas exchange, and food oxidation. Films and coating consisting of a matrix of biopolymers are constant phases because they provide nanoemulsion droplets with monodispersity and durability. Droplet coalescence is decreased by the continuous phase's increased viscosity (Artiga-Artigas et al., 2017). The bioactive components are dispersed in a continuous phase that forms the matrix of the packaging films as part of the creation of nanoemulsion edible films. After adding food-grade emulsions, the mixture is homogenized using the appropriate high or low-energy procedures. The homogenized mixture is cast into controlled-thickness films and allowed to dry. The structural, morphological, thermal, mechanical, and barrier properties of these films are also described (Otoni et al., 2014). The functional qualities of the nanoemulsions are also maintained in effect by the biopolymers utilized in their creation. Additionally, the items were coated with nanoemulsion formulations to test their potency as coating agents without the need of a polymer matrix. A chitosan solution that contains nanoemulsions of mandarin essential oil has been used to coat green beans (Donsì et al., 2015; Artiga-Artigas et al., 2017).

The essential oils of plants such as thyme, oregano, cloves, and orange demonstrate a strong antimicrobial activity against foodborne infections with their constituents like thymol, carvacrol, eugenol, limonene, and cinnamon. Their hydrophobicity, however, restricts their use in the food matrix. Utilising essential oil nanoemulsion compositions as natural preservatives in foods and in food packaging can solve this issue (Alexandre et al., 2016). Foodborne pathogens like Listeria monocytogenes, Salmonella typhimurium, and E. coli O157:H7 were prevented from growing on fresh lettuce by the use of oregano oil nanoemulsions. Bacterial membranes can be damaged by oregano oil nanoemulsions, according to research by Bhargava et al. (2015). According to Sugumar et al. (2015), orange oil nanoemulsions prevented Saccharomyces cerevisiae from degrading apple juice.

Clove bud and oregano essential oil nanoemulsions with 180–250 nm droplets improved antibacterial activity on edible methylcellulose films. They preserved sliced bread by suppressing yeast and mould growth (Otoni et al., 2014). Ginger essential oil nanoemulsions improve gelatin-based active food packaging films (Alexandre et al., 2016). Curcumin nanoemulsions with pectin edible coatings and mean droplet diameters of 40 nm preserved chilled chicken for 12 days at 4 °C. By reducing the growth of yeast, mould, and psychrophiles, nanoemulsion aids in the prevention of bacterial deterioration. Furthermore, it had lower levels of total volatile nitrogen and thiobarbituric acid, higher sensory ratings, and a higher water-holding capacity than the control (Abdou et al., 2018). Nanoemulsions may be used to prepare food sustainably. They may protect the active components against changes in pH, oxidation, temperature, and enzymatic activities. Nanoemulsions are used in food items created by firms such as Nestle and Unilever, as well as a few startups (Silva et al., 2012; Salvia-Trujillo et al., 2019). Natural antioxidant nanoemulsions (derived from fruits, vegetables, or grains) have been patented for use as food preservatives. Fresh and minimally processed foods may be preserved by using freeze-dried nanoemulsions containing naturally encapsulated antioxidants.

International Flavors & Fragrances Inc. has obtained a patent for a flavour nanoemulsion that consists of hydrophobic oil droplets with taste, an aqueous phase, and a surfactant system that includes lecithin and a polyethoxylated sorbitan fatty acid ester. A flavor nanoemulsion has been used to make alcoholic beverages as well as other optically clear liquid beverages (Lee et al., 2017, p. 595).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Commercialization of nanoemulsion in food products
ce:section.ce:para: Nestlé, Unilever, and other startups use nano emulsifiers in food. Nestle uses nanoemulsions in its ice creams to cut fat from 16% to 1% (Unilever, 2011). RBC Life Sciences created Nanoceuticals Slim Shake Chocolate. NanoCluster delivery blends tiny dietary nutrients into a powder. LivOn Labs' liposomal Nanospheres contain Vitamin C in Lypo-Spheric Vitamin C (Aswathanarayan & Vittal, 2019).

The nanoemulsions are thermodynamic and kinetically undistorted and there is no aggregation, clumping, creamling or coalescence. It is non-toxic and can transport hydrophilic and lipophilic chemicals. The rate of absorption is increased due to its nano size. The addition of nanoemulsion increases the barrier qualities of films in the food packaging business.

There are certain drawbacks to nanoemulsions, such as Ostwald ripening, the need for a high number of surfactants or cosurfactants, and the fact that stability can be changed by environmental parameters such as temperature and pH (Mason et al., 2006).

The past few years have seen a significant increase in interest in CDs due to a number of factors, including their low cost, decreased toxicity, chemical inertness, water solubility, higher compatibility and electrical conductivity, photostability, and distinctive physicochemical and optical characteristics with significant potential as detection sensors (Qu et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2020; Eskalen, Çesme, Kerli, & Özgan, 2021). Low energy efficiency, synthetic circumstances, and a complicated manufacturing procedure are the main obstacles restricting the use of carbon dots (Eskalen et al., 2021). Carbon dots are fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with a diameter of less than 10 nm. Since their first discovery in 2004 by Xu and colleagues, carbon dots have been the subject of extensive study. Currently, efforts are being made to comprehend their application-related characteristics (Qu et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2020; Eskalen et al., 2021). Carbon dots are made up of polymer dots, complanate graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and quasi-spherical carbon quantum dots (CQDs), with CQDs being the most extensively researched for the detection of food safety and food quality (Qu et al., 2018). The majority of carbon dots have significant UV light absorption (). When characterised, C-dots are considered to be devoid of metals. The chemical structure of carbon dots comprises carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, and oxygen (Bottini et al., 2006) as shown in Fig. 3 .

Carbon dots can be synthesized by two methods: the top-down method and the bottom-up method. Both methods are different from each other in terms of reaction conditions, processing time, and raw material cost (Qu et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2020; Eskalen et al., 2021). Based on their method of synthesis, they can be called graphene nanodots (top-down method) or carbon nanodots (bottom-up method) (Tuerhong et al., 2017) (Fig. 3). The breakdown of carbon using physical and chemical methods to obtain nano-scale molecules is termed as a top-down method. On the other hand, the bottom-up method employs smaller molecules for the release of carbon dots ().

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of C-dots in food systems
ce:section.ce:para: To employ any new material or substance for technological applications, it is very important to understand its chemical and physical characteristics and properties (Rossi et al., 2017). After their surprising origination, carbon dots are being utilized in the food and dairy industry in numerous ways. They are being studied for their different properties and characteristics and their different applications are being explored. Some of these applications are discussed in the following sub-sections sections (Table 4 ).

To guarantee the safety of food, there has been a lot of focus worldwide on detecting foods with contaminants and nutritional ingredients rapidly (Qu et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2020; Eskalen et al., 2021). For the analysis of food safety, the use of highly selective and sensitive detection strategies is being studied. Because of their superior properties, carbon dots represent an excellent potential to extend beyond more advanced technologies (Luo et al., 2020; Han et al., 2022).

In a study performed by Eskalen et al. (2021), blue fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized from rosemary leaves and were used for studying their efficiency as an antioxidant and latent fingerprint detection under UV light. The application of CDs in combination with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was found to be effective in preventing the spoilage of bananas for up to 19 days compared to untreated samples and samples treated with only PVA coating. It also showed inhibition of about 8–10 mm diameter zone against Salmonella typhimurium, Escherichia coli, Bacillus subtilis, Staphylococcus aureus, and Candida albicans.

Yan et al. (2016) synthesized fluorescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) using a hydrothermal process and used them as a fluorescent probe for the detection of aluminum ion (Al3+) and dopamine in food samples which are of concern during food safety monitoring. There was a liner response between the relative fluorescence intensity and dopamine ions at a concentration of 20–80 μM with 0.0078 μM limit of detection when CNDs were used and in the case of aluminum, ions showed a linear response at a concentration of 4–40 μM, with Al3+ having a detection limit of 0.0329 μM on using CNDs in combination with dopamine. Al3+ when combined with dopamine, improved the recovery of CNDs due to the stronger binding between the two. The above-synthesized CNDs were of low cytotoxicity and had good biocompatibility which makes them a promising tool for biological imaging and food security in the upcoming future.

Several well-developed technologies such as HPLC, GC-MS, LC-MS, ELISA, etc. have been used for the detection of various food and dairy components and to assess their risk factors in them because of their accuracy and sensitivity. Due to their time-consuming nature, complicated sample preparation, and high equipment and analysis cost, there is a limitation in their practical uses. Carbon dots are being studied for their sensing properties to overcome the above-mentioned drawbacks of different modern technologies and their role as effective sensing tools (Han et al., 2022). Some of the studies have been discussed below:

Vijeata et al. (2022) studied fluorescence carbon dots from Indian bael patra using the hydrothermal carbonization process as a sensing tool for the detection of the perilous food colorant “Allura Red”. Carbon dots extracted from the outer shell (C-CDs) of the bael were highly efficient in detecting Allura red is superior to CDs generated from edible pulp (P-CDs) and a combination of pulp and gum (M-CD-s) due to a larger ratio of surface-active adsorption sites and a quenching efficiency of 94.10%. These CDs when used as co-adsorbates for both Allura red and ferrous ions, showed the limit of detection in the range of 0.166, 0.260, 0.607, and 0.162, 0.142, 0.144 respectively. The extracted CDs were found to be highly selective towards Allura red and ferrous ions and were reusable even after 10 subsequent recycling, making CDs a noble scavenger for food colorants as well as potential application in waste water treatment.

Tang et al. (2021) created nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon plates for the detection of curcumin and iodide in food samples such as tomato juice, mustard, ginger paste, curry powder, and chili powder, as well as complicated biological samples such as urine. These NCDs were synthesized in a single step employing a solvent-free solid phase technique using urea and tartaric acid as precursors. After forming a mercuric iodide (HgI2) complex, curcumin was detected at 29.8 nM and iodide at 69.4 nM, presenting a promising approach for food and environmental monitoring with remarkable sensitivity.

Dai et al. (2014) used a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system between gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and CDs to create a carbon-based biosensor to detect melamine in milk. The devised method demonstrated enhanced fluorescence in the presence of melamine with a limit of detection of 36 nM, making the approach favorable due to its high sensitivity, cheap cost, and quick analysis time.

The above studies show the effectiveness of carbon dots as sensors and can be constructively used for the detection of several types of additives or heavy metals present in food products. Detection of these components demands very complex sample preparation with sophisticated instrumentation which can be overcome by using CDs. Carbon dots have good photostability and luminance performance over other modern technologies. The low-toxic nature of the CDs with excellent catalytic properties makes them a potential bio-sensing tool (Tang et al., 2021). There is still a need for improvement in their mechanism and extraction techniques to overcome the challenges in their application in the analytical fields with improved functionality (Cui et al., 2021; Qu et al., 2018).

To improve water barrier, thermal stability, and mechanical properties which will ultimately lead to an improvement in the shelf life of goods packed inside them, CDs are being used for packaging materials (Tang et al., 2021). They are used in combination with several other kinds of materials such as chitosan, carboxymethyl cellulose, polyvinyl alcohol, soy protein isolate, etc. (Zhou et al., 2022). The surface charge on the carbon dots, their shape and size, and the functional groups attached to them are the deciding factor for their antimicrobial property.

Travlou et al. (2018) studied the efficacy of sulfur- and nitrogen-doped carbons quantum dots (S-CQDs and N-CQDs) in packaging material for their bactericidal activity against gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria. It was found that N-CQDs with a surface charge of −6.5 mV were superior to S-CQDs with a surface charge of −47.2 mV in inhibiting E. coli and B. subitilis.

Heteroatom-Doped CDs (nitrogen, phosphorous, and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NPSC-dots)) were immobilized on nonpolar plastic film (polypropylene) using photo grafting polymerization technique to study the effectiveness of the developed packaging material in enhancing storage stability of food items. The NPSC-dots were coated to a thickness of ∼25 μm for improving the radical scavenging activity and antifogging properties of the film. NPSC-dot-coated PP film had superior antifogging behavior over the control sample and poly (acrylic acid)-coated PP film when subjected to a laboratory simulated method at 60 °C for 120 min. Bananas packed in PP/NPSC-dots film were stable for up to 5 days without any black discoloration and blemishes compared to the control and sample packed in only PP film. DPPH assay showed 82%/100 mg of inhibition in the case of NPSC-dot-coated PP films (Das et al., 2021).

Bottom-up approach: This method produces C-dots that are less time-consuming, easier to operate, have a very low production cost, and can be easily scaled up to a large scale. They are eco-friendly and lack toxicity.
The size distribution is broad, having a low yield, poor size control, and few small precursors available.

Top-down approach: This method produces C-dots with several advantages, including the ability to fabricate in a variety of gases, controllable size, photoluminescence property, ease of breaking large carbon materials, low crystalline structure, and well dispersion in the medium (Cui et al., 2021; Qu et al., 2018).
The production requires high costs and energy, needs sophisticated instruments, and undergoes an extensive purification process (Cui et al., 2021; Qu et al., 2018).

Due to its tunability and ability to handle food ingredients, nanoparticle (NPs) manufacturing has garnered interest in the food sector (Nie et al., 2021; Krasitskaya et al., 2022). Nanoparticles may be organic or inorganic. Inorganic NPs include metals and their oxides, such as titanium dioxide, silicon dioxide, and zinc oxide, may be crystalline or amorphous, spherical or non-spherical, and have various surface properties. Natural product nanoparticles such as proteins, lipids, and/or carbohydrates are used to create organic materials (McClements, 2005). These nanoparticles have demonstrated significant potential and capacity in every aspect of the food sector, for instance; organic nanoparticles can be used to create food-grade polymers, nanoemulsions, and nano-encapsulations (Bajpai et al., 2018a). While the inorganic nanoparticles find use in food systems as functional ingredients or as sources of bioavailable nutrients. Given below are discussions of some of the organic and inorganic nanoparticles (Table 5 ).

Fe2O3, among which superparamagnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles are the most often employed magnetic nanoparticles in food systems because they are nontoxic, biocompatible, and do not maintain any residual magnetism once the external magnetic field is removed. Because of their strong chemical activity and ease of oxidation in the air (especially magnetite), bare iron oxide nanoparticles lose their magnetic characteristics and dispersibility in general (Okoli et al., 2011; Krasitskaya et al., 2022). To ensure the stability of magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles, a suitable surface coating and efficient protective measures are required. These approaches include grafting or coating with organic molecules such as biomolecules, polymers, or surfactants, as well as coating with silica, metal oxide, or sulfur dioxide (Wu et al., 2022; Nie et al., 2021).

According to Góral et al. (2023), the production of high-quality iron oxide nanoparticles may be accomplished by a variety of processes, including co-precipitation, thermal breakdown, hydrothermal synthesis, microemulsion, and sonochemical synthesis. Producing Fe3O4 or c- Fe2O3 using this method is by far the most popular approach. In this method, ferric and ferrous ions are mixed in a 1:2 M ratio in highly basic solutions at ambient temperature or at increased temperatures, depending on the circumstance. The proportions of ferric and ferrous ions, the temperature of the reaction, the pH value, the level of ionic strength in the medium, and the kind of salt used all have an impact on the size and shape of the iron oxide nanoparticles (Al-Baldawi et al., 2019). The technique generates particles with a broad particle size distribution, which results in a wide range of neutralising temperatures due to the fact that TB is dependent on the particle size of the product. The process of thermal decomposition is a complicated one that begins with the transformation of iron into N-nitroso phenylhydroxylamine, acetylacetonate, and Fe(CO)5, and then continues with the oxidation of the substance at high temperatures. Crystallization of compounds must take place in a closed container at high temperatures (generally between 130 and 250 °C) and high vapour pressures (normally between 0.3 and 4 MPa). This is the hydrothermal method of chemical synthesis. Co-precipitation is a technique that is often used to manufacture water-soluble and biocompatible iron oxide nanoparticles (Góral et al., 2023). Thermal breakdown and the hydrothermal synthetic approach are also good methods for changing the size and structure of nanoparticles.

Magnetic nanoparticles have received interest as potential enzyme immobilization agents. They have a high enzyme loading capacity and can easily gather enzymes from complicated mixtures using an external magnetic field. Furthermore, the techniques of preparation are easy and aid in the acquisition of desirable physical qualities (Cao et al., 2012; Kamran et al., 2019). Covalent bonding and physical adsorption are the main methods for immobilising enzymes on magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles. A high-binding magnetic nanoparticle may be covalently bonded (Yu et al., 2021). Physical adsorption is based on hydrogen or ionic interactions with enzymes. However, for industrial applications, this approach has a disadvantage since enzymes quickly slip off the magnetic particle when subjected to minute modifications (Cao et al., 2012).

Numerous studies have been conducted on the use of Fe2O3 nanoparticles for enzyme immobilization. Xiao and Mujumdar (2020) mobilized P. carrageenovora CICC 23819 κ-carrageenase onto carboxyl-functionalized Fe2O3 magnetic nanoparticles. The enzyme immobilized showed an enzyme recovery rate of 46.9%, respectively. Masi et al. (2018) showed enhanced activity of protease after immobilization with Fe2O3 nanoparticles. The protease was produced from the bacterial species Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Enterococcus hirae which were secluded from the dairy industry effluents and demonstrated an immobilization capacity of 6000 μM/g. Afzal et al. (2020) demonstrated the immobilization of Candida antarctica B lipase (Cal-B) on gallic acid resin grafted with magnetic Fe2O3 nanoparticles with 91% activity retention.

Fe2O3 nanoparticles have been used in protein purification to replace time-consuming and costly methods such as dialysis, precipitation, chromatography, ultrafiltration, and centrifugation (Waghmode et al., 2019; Blaznik et al., 2021; Youn and Choi, 2022). It works on the core idea of magnetic affinity separation via direct or indirect modes. In the direct mode of action, magnetic nanoparticles are linked to ligands that grab target proteins from crude samples. In the indirect method, the ligands are introduced to the crude sample, which forms a complex with the target protein. This complex is then targeted and collected by a magnetic nanoparticle (Kamran et al., 2019; Yu et al., 2021).

Okoli et al. (2011) used citrate-coated superparamagnetic Fe2O3 nanoparticles to purify coagulant protein from Moringa oleifera. They discovered that commercial microbeads had 20% less coagulation activity than citrate-coated iron oxide nanoparticles in the first elution step. Starch-coated Fe2O3 magnetic nanoparticles (starch-MNPs) were made and tested as an affinity sorbent for removing maltose-binding protein (MBP) from recombinant proteins. They demonstrated great binding capacity, stability, and the potential to be employed more than once (Krasitskaya et al., 2022).

ZnO is generally used as a zinc supplement because zinc is an essential mineral for the body. Because zinc deficiency is common in children and adults with dietary problems or GI tract disorders, these individuals require zinc supplements or zinc-fortified foods. Zinc oxide nanoparticles reportedly have higher efficiency than supplements such as zinc gluconate, sulfate, acetate, and citrate (Gudkov et al., 2021; Moradpoor et al., 2021). The Food and Drug Administration has recognized zinc oxide nanoparticles as GRAS substances (Youn and Choi, 2022). Besides the use of supplements, ZnO NPs have been reported to have antibacterial potential. Tayel et al. (2011) demonstrated antibacterial action against L. monocytogenes, Salmonella enteritidis, and E. coli O157:H7 in liquid egg white and culture media, respectively. The possible antibacterial effect may be attributed to the distortion and damage to the bacterial cell membrane, which causes intracellular contents to leak out and eventually causes the bacterial cell to die (Sirelkhatim et al., 2015).

Titanium dioxide (TiO2, titania, and titanium (IV) oxide) has a wide range of real-world and potential uses. Due to its brightness and opacifying capacity, it is mostly employed as a pigment (hiding power). TiO2 has outstanding thermal stability, which makes it an excellent candidate for absorption or scattering of UV radiation, as well as resistance to chemical attacks. The range of TiO2's utility has increased thanks to the production of nano-scaled TiO2 particles (Musial et al., 2020).

TiO2 NPs are primarily produced via a variety of chemical synthesis procedures, including the sol-gel method, the solvo-thermal approach, the co-precipitation method, and the hydrothermal method. NP synthesis was done in an autoclave at high pressures and temperatures (Musial et al., 2020). Solvothermal employs non-aqueous solvents, whereas hydrothermal uses aqueous solvents. Although the chemical approach to manufacturing TiO2 NPs is popular because it is easy to implement and provides for fine control of NP size and shape. In addition, the utilization of reducing agents taken from biological sources such as fruit extract, plant waste, and microorganisms has been investigated as an alternate method for the production of nanoparticles (Irshad et al., 2021). TiO2 NPs, also known as E171, are found in paints, plastics, personal care products, and food (Musial et al., 2020; Blaznik et al., 2021). TiO2 NPs have an extremely low removal rate in addition to concentrating, accumulating, and enlarging in the tissues of humans and other vertebrates. Some studies have shown that in young mice, symptoms of heart damage, liver emphysema, and non-allergic mast cell activation in stomach tissue have been observed.

However, mature animals have shown only mild toxic effects. Therefore, given that youngsters are thought to ingest noticeably more TiO2 NPs than adults do, this discovery is especially significant. Currently, France has chosen to reconsider the safety of TiO2 (E171) as a food additive at the legislative level (Siddiqui & Alrumman, 2021). TiO2 NPs use is restricted in India to 0.01% (w/w) in drinks and 1% (w/w) in chewing gum (Waghmode et al., 2019).

Lipids are natural substances that include fats, waxes, mono-, di-, and triglycerides. Nowadays, these lipids are transformed into lipid nanoparticles to develop colloidal-based delivery systems, which include solid lipid nanoparticles, liposomes, emulsions, and nanostructured lipid carriers. These nanostructures are hence being used to encapsulate bioactive components (McClements, 2005).

Using lipid nanoparticles for the encapsulation of bioactive compounds has certain advantages. Firstly, they increase the bioavailability of the compound. Secondly, being optically transparent, they are desirable to be used in foods such as soups or beverages (Blanco-Llamero et al., 2022). Thirdly, with encapsulation, the physical stability of the nanoparticles is increased (Weiss et al., 2008).

Solid lipid nanoparticles are the most preferred lipid-based colloidal delivery systems for hydrophobic bioactive compounds. They consist of a solid lipid core stabilized by a surfactant (Blanco-Llamero et al., 2022). A solid lipid is a combination of solid lipids that are solid at 65 °C. The bioactive components are embedded in the solid lipids (Lu et al., 2021; Shukat & Relkin, 2011).

The properties and structure of solid lipid nanoparticles are influenced by the parent carrier chosen for the encapsulation of bioactive compounds. Upon solidifying the liquid lipid, the crystal generated upon cooling will determine the characteristics of the release of the encapsulated material. This also influences the loading capacity of the bioactive component. Triacylglycerols (TAGs) or diacylglycerols (DAGs) and phospholipids are the most commonly used lipid carriers. Other than that, their crystallization rate and temperature are significant factors that affect SLN stability. The produced nanoparticles are susceptible to deterioration if the required precautions are not followed during manufacturing to promote controlled crystallization in time (Blanco-Llamero et al., 2022). Considering SLN particle size impacts organoleptic qualities including appearance, stability, texture, and mouthfeel, it is a crucial factor for developing delivery methods for bioactive (Shukat & Relkin, 2011).

Also, the type of surfactant used has a big effect on the size, surface charge, stability, polymorphic transition, and other physical and chemical properties of solid lipid nanoparticles. Calculating a surfactant's hydrophile-lipophile balance HLB value is typically how surfactants are chosen. The balance between the size and tensile strength of the hydrophilic and lipophilic groups in a surfactant molecule determines the presence of HLB (Weiss et al., 2008).

The ideal surfactant must control crystallization as well as protect the SLN from physical instability. Ionic surfactants that stabilize SLN by preventing aggregation and flocculation are typically employed in conjunction with non-ionic surfactants that offer stability against recrystallization. Ultrasonication, multiple emulsion techniques, solvent injection micro-emulsion techniques, and high-pressure homogenization are just a few of the methods used to create solid lipid nanoparticles (Da Silva Santos et al., 2019).

Homogenization with high pressure is most prevalent to create liquid NPs. Melted solid or oil dissolve or disseminate the active component in this method. By driving the liquid at high pressure (10–200 MPa) through a tiny hole (a few microns wide), the melt is homogenized, and size gets reduced to submicron (Desfrançois et al., 2018).

Cold homogenization avoids active ingredient degradation caused by heat homogenization. Several issues with hot homogenization could be addressed by cold homogenization, namely the breakdown of active ingredients or diffusion into the aqueous phase caused by temperature. In contrast to hot homogenization, cold homogenized samples often exhibit bigger particle sizes and broader size dispersion. On the application side, lipid NPs have been used to enclose, safeguard, and release hydrophobic bioactive, including colors, tastes, antimicrobials, antioxidants, minerals, and nutraceuticals (Da Silva Santos et al., 2019).

Overall, the use of these nanomaterials is widely employed as carriers for food improvement, such as nanoemulsion and nanoencapsulation, preservatives, animal feed, taste or color additives, and preservative systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantages and limitations of nanoparticles in food
ce:section.ce:para: Longer shelf life, additional taste variants, and health-promoting additives are a few advantages of nanoparticle technology (Sekhon, 2010; Desfrançois et al., 2018).

The effects of nanoparticles on human organs depend on their physical and chemical properties, such as their biological availability and spread, concentration in food products, and the amount of food eaten (Kamran et al., 2019; Nie et al., 2021). DNA damage, Parkinson's syndrome, Alzheimer's disease, and inflammation of the gastrointestinal system have all been associated to nanoparticle exposure in human studies. They may also cause oxidative stress in the cells of the human body and migrate from the lungs to other parts of the body, including the blood, the nucleus of cells, and the central nervous system. Naseer et al. (2018) have found that being around nanoparticles for a long time can hurt the kidney, liver, and other important organs. Besides, studies on the absorption of nanoparticles need attention. For instance, the enzymes in the GIT are unable to digest some kinds of lipid nanoparticles that are now used in meals, either due to the use of indigestible oil phases such as terpenes or hydrocarbons in the oil or to the inhibition of digestive enzymes by the interfacial layer coating on oil droplets (McClements & Rao, 2011; Desfrançois et al., 2018). Different chemical processes used in the synthesis of nanoparticles have negative side effects and produce environmentally harmful byproducts that adversely pollute the environment (Yu et al., 2021). So, even though nanotechnology has a lot of benefits, its future in food processing is still uncertain because of its toxicity, rules and regulations, public awareness, and public acceptability (Bajpai et al., 2018a; Moradpoor et al., 2021).

Proteins are a rich source of the various amino acids required for different metabolic reactions. They are a source of even those amino acids that are important but are not produced by the human metabolic system. Food proteins have many functions, including the ability to help stabilize or produce foams, emulsions, films, sols, and so on. Other functionalities include a) influencing rheological behavior, b) sensory properties, and c) TE for determining the secondary structures and structural features of conjugates (Nooshkam et al., 2020).

However, proteins are extremely sensitive to high temperatures, pH fluctuations, protein degrading agents, organic solvents, media composition, flow rate or shear effect, etc. The sensitivity of proteins to such intrinsic factors affects their functional properties and restricts their industrial applications. Different physio-chemical strategies are being developed to enhance the stability of proteins in adverse environmental conditions and improve their technological properties. However, chemical methods are associated with the use and incorporation of chemicals harmful to the human system (Kumar et al., 2022; Ma et al., 2020). In this regard, the Millard reaction (MR) is preferred for the production of food conjugates since it does not involve any external chemical agents and takes place in natural and controlled conditions (de Oliveira et al., 2016). MR is a cascade of non-enzymatic condensation reactions between proteins and reducing sugars that produce protein-polysaccharide conjugates. In MR, a covalent bond is introduced between the amine group of proteins and the carbonyl group of reducing sugars, generating different Millard reaction products (MRPs) like melanoidin and other heterocyclic products, aldehydes, and reductive ketones. Generally, Maillard-type protein-potassium conjugates are generated by controlled heat treatment either in an aqueous solution (wet-heating method) or a dry state (dry-heating method) (Sun et al., 2022). Polysaccharides are used in MR due to their stability, abundance, and diversity, as well as their economic, hydrophilic, non-toxic, and biodegradable natures (Liu et al., 2020). It is pertinent to mention that the properties of conjugate proteins, glycation rate, and type and amount of MRPs produced depend on the nature and ratio of proteins and polysaccharides, reaction time, pH, temperature, etc. The biological and functional qualities of proteins are improved by MR. Polysaccharide-Maillard-Type Protein Different methods are used to determine the secondary structures of conjugates, including Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), dynamic light scattering (DLS), circular dichroism (CD), size distribution, and sodium dodecyl sulfate polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) (Sun et al., 2022). The enormous stability of MRPs against adverse environmental conditions like pH fluctuations, high temperatures, organic solvents, etc. makes them a favorable tool for the encapsulation as well as delivery of different bioactive compounds, oils, etc. in food as well as pharmaceutical industries (Nooshkam & Varidi, 2020).

Different MRPs are often used as functional food ingredients because they improve the taste, texture, and other physical properties of food products. Protein-sugar conjugates (Maillard-type conjugates) have better applicability in terms of food encapsulation and delivery of bioactive compounds than parent proteins or peptides since they are resistant to adverse environmental conditions. The wider application of protein conjugates in encapsulation and delivery systems is due to their stable and improved technological properties. Different forms of protein-polysaccharide conjugate-based delivery systems include nanoparticle-based delivery systems, hydrogels, microcapsules, and emulsions (nano, multiple, multi-layered, Pickering emulsions, etc.) (Wei & Huang, 2019).

Polyphenols (curcumin, resveratrol), carotenoids (lycopene, lutein, and beta-carotene), proteins and bioactive peptides (lactoferrin, casein hydrolysate), vitamins (folic acid), essential oils, and minerals (iron) are all delivered via protein-polysaccharide-based delivery vehicles. Through a process known as the Maillard reaction, Wang et al. (2016) were able to create bovine serum albumin-dextran conjugates that were loaded with curcumin. The oil-in-water emulsion improved both the long-term stability of curcumin as well as its oral bioavailability. In a work that was quite similar to this one, Gumus et al. (2016) generated an oil-in-water emulsion of a casein-dextran conjugate that was loaded with lutein, which is a natural pigment or nutraceutical. According to the findings of the study, conjugation enhanced lutein's physiochemical stability while having no effect on the compound's bioaccessibility. Because of their increased stability, accessibility, and prolonged release, protein–polysaccharide conjugates as carriers have demonstrated a promising response towards the encapsulation and delivery of food components (Sun et al., 2022). The conjugation process is plagued by a number of issues, the most significant of which is that the use of freeze-drying as a pre-treatment is not possible from an economic standpoint. Eichhorn et al. (2023) demonstrated the use of vacuum drying as an economically viable strategy for the plant protein-pectin conjugation with better functionality. This was done so that the problem could be circumvented. Researchers concluded that the produced conjugates might be used successfully as emulsifiers in food systems.

Maillard-type conjugates are being widely used for the fabrication of edible films employed for food packaging. It is believed that MR can make a bigger difference in food safety and quality as related research and understanding of MR grow. Fig. 4 shows the scientific content and overall view of the food conjugates. For example, egg white protein is commercially used for the development of films owing to its film-forming potential and edible nature. However, it is associated with the disadvantage of low water barrier features, which is overcome by the glycation process using xylose. The egg white protein-xylose conjugate is used for coating walnuts since it shows less water permeability in comparison to the control (Li et al., 2012).

Another class of conjugates, commonly known as “protein-phenol conjugates,” affects the functional, nutraceutical, and sensory features of food ingredients. Such conjugates are formed both by covalent and non-covalent interactions. The functionalities attributed to protein-phenol conjugates could include improved solubility, emulsifying capacity, thermal stability, gelling properties, etc. Such conjugates could be used as food additives to enhance the quality and other functionalities (Quan et al., 2019).

Lipid-protein conjugates are another class of conjugates that involve both lipids and proteins in a single conjugation. The conjugates are widely used as advanced novel delivery systems due to their amalgamation of both lipid- and protein-specific properties. Such conjugates are reported to have advanced properties, viz., sustained release, decrease in toxicity, increase in therapeutic efficacy, enhanced targeted delivery, etc. (Dissanayake et al., 2022). Chemical conjugation involves the formation of an amide bond between the amine group of proteins and the carboxyl group of lipids, the use of desolvating substances to encapsulate drugs and conjugate proteins and lipids (desolvation), the encapsulation of drug-encapsulated NPs in a lipid bilayer (lipid film hydration), the coating of positively charged NPs with negatively charged proteins and vice versa (electrostatic coating), and the formation of small droplets (Dissanayake et al., 2022). The synthesis of lipid-protein conjugates is characterised by DLS (particle size determination), zeta potential (particle stability), SEM and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) (conjugate morphology), DSC, FTIR, and XRD (physical attributes of encapsulated molecules, variation in functional groups, conjugation driving forces). Aside from their role in drug and gene delivery, lipid-protein conjugates have been widely used as an efficient delivery system for nutraceuticals (Banerjee & Kundu, 2018).

Hapten-protein conjugates play an important role in detection of environmental and food contaminants. The non-immunogenic nature of the small molecules (hapten) favours the development of immunogenic complex with macromolecules like proteins to elicit an immune response for their detection. The production of specific antibodies against haptens depends on the hapten-protein conjugate characteristics (Li et al., 2017). In an attempt, Rajesh et al. (2013) developed an immunoassay format to quantify the hapten–protein conjugation density of 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid and atrazine (pesticide classes) coupled to carrier protein. Likewise, Shao et al. (2021) conjugated amino groups of the hapten with carrier proteins in presence of coupling agent carbonyldiimidazole for the detection of albendazole and its metabolites in milk and tissue.

Liu et al. (2018) encapsulated curcumin and resveratrol in core-shell nanoparticles using zein-epigallocatechin gallate conjugated (zein-ECGCs) and coated them with rhamnolipid. The generated system demonstrated enhanced water solubility, bioavailability, bioaccessibility, and stability of both curcumin and resveratrol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantages and limitations of food conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: For the purposes of food quality and safety, proteins-polysaccharide conjugates that are produced by MR or combinations of various lipid and protein characteristics may find application in the food industry. Overall, the conjugated substances are capable of being emulsifiers and stabilizers in an emulsion system (Nooshkam et al., 2023).

There has only been a moderate amount of investigation on the structural and functional connections between proteins, polysaccharides, and lipids. According to Bealer et al. (2020), in order to exert control over the progress of the reaction, it is required to understand the properties of the biopolymers involved and to choose the suitable circumstances.

In terms of food quality and safety, those advance materials were also able to create niche markets around the world. In order to preserve and prevent spoilage or contamination, as well as prolonging shelf life, these functional materials play both a role as food ingredients and an agent for protection. In fact, both industry and scientific communities as well as food manufacturing organizations have paid particular attention to these materials. Over the past few years, in view of increasing research attention and modernistic and advanced approaches towards preserving food quality and maintaining food safety, functional foods materials have evolved. Fig. 5 shows how the above materials will be used in the future to make sure that food is safe and secure.

Aerogels in food are a promising study area. Aerogels are not yet mentioned in EU food rules. Thus, volatile residue safety and aerogel manufacture must be considered. Bio-based aerogels require highly clean fractions, making them pricey. Finally, new tools must be developed to determine the nature and dynamics of aerogel-biological tissue interaction. These issues should be resolved in the future years, making aerogels a viable food business choice.

Since nanoemulsion may deliver bioactive chemicals and functional food components, it has several food and beverage applications. Until their production costs are profitable and they meet food industry safety requirements, food-grade nanoemulsions won't be extensively employed. Increasing bioactivity of encapsulated components is critical for manufacturing. Designing effective products with solid particles, micelles, polymeric structures, and dissolved species requires a thorough grasp of nanoemulsion compatibility. Depending on nanoemulsion composition and interaction with other colloidal dispersions, outcomes may vary. Nanoemulsions are promising soft-matter systems with high-value applications in fundamental research, colloidal design, and biotechnology.

CDs have garnered interest due to their exceptional brightness, minimal toxicity, and compatibility with living organisms. CDs-based nanosensors will improve food quality and safety in the future. The stated research shows that CDs are still young, and their photoluminescence process is yet unknown. Synthesis methods are versatile and there are many precursors, but they are still in the lab. The quantum yield and luminous characteristics decrease due to further purification, which takes a lengthy time. CDs with other technologies might improve analytical performance and expand applications. When immunoassay methodologies and sensor technologies are developed and employed in food quality and safety, they might be integrated with carbon dots to create a food safety platform.

The degree of human exposure to nanoparticles is highly dependent on the specific application and concentration of use in the food business, with exposure risk being higher in domains where nanomaterials are introduced directly to food items as carriers of new food additives. So, the public should be taught about the effects of nanotechnology on health, safety, and the environment as it is used and developed in the food chain. For the time being, we may conclude that there are just a few research studies that provide consistent and valuable toxicity results. Most toxicity tests have been conducted at considerably greater doses than the practical dose. However, most hazardous compounds are tolerable in tiny amounts and dangerous only when eaten in large quantities. Validation and evidence-based research still differ. The dissolving capabilities of diverse nanoparticles in processed foods and biological systems must thus be investigated since they can be crucial determinants in determining their fates and possible oral toxicity. In a variety of applications, green synthesis removes the negative impacts of the generated NPs. Because fewer precursors are used during green synthesis, it has been demonstrated to be more effective than chemically synthesized nanoparticles. Green nanoparticle production requires more research as fewer plants are needed.

Due to their stability, solubility, emulsifying abilities, and biological features, food conjugates have opened a new chapter in food and pharmaceutical delivery system research. They have been developed as potent emulsifiers, encapsulants, gelling agents, antimicrobials, antioxidants, anti-browning compounds, etc. However, it is pertinent to mention that in vivo (cell line and animal model) and clinical studies are required to validate their properties as demonstrated by in vitro studies. To expand the applications of food conjugates, safety, and toxicity concerns must be addressed following government regulations. Furthermore, thorough investigations are required to evaluate the stability of food conjugates in the gastrointestinal tract using modern interventions to improve the techno-functionality of conjugates as a delivery system.

Food security challenges encompass ensuring that all humans have access to sufficient food for a productive life. However, a crucial aspect of food security lies in addressing safety concerns from chemical, physiological, and biotechnological perspectives. While there are many facets to food security, this article focuses on discussing advanced engineered materials, their fundamentals, and their applications in the realm of food safety and security. The food market's evolving demands for fresh, authentic, convenient, and flavorful products, coupled with the need for improved food quality and safety, necessitate the involvement of various stakeholders such as farmers, consumers, researchers, government agencies, and consumer advocacy groups. These entities influence food safety policies and advocate for sustainable food production practises. It is essential to raise awareness among these interested parties and provide them with information about advanced engineered materials. This empowers them to make data-driven decisions regarding food consumption as well as adopt policies and guidelines that safeguard food safety and promote environmental sustainability.

Resolving these challenges requires ongoing scientific research to inform regulations, practises, and the application of modern technologies for increasing food production, improving sustainability, and assessing their impact on the environment. In order to enhance food safety, security, and sustainability, it is crucial to fund innovative research and encourage collaboration that generates new data, techniques, and perspectives. Overall, addressing food security concerns necessitates a multidimensional approach that involves the cooperation and support of various stakeholders, ongoing scientific research, and the adoption of advanced engineered materials. By investing in these areas, we can make progress in overcoming the challenges and ensuring a more secure and sustainable food system.

Arun Kumar Gupta, Conceptualization, Supervision, Resources, Writing-original draft preparation, Reviewing and Editing, Pratiksha, Writing-original draft preparation, Himanshu Kumar, Tanuva Das, Rajeev Ranjan, Resources, Writing-original draft preparation, Sarushi Rastogi, Sadhna Mishra, Writing-original draft preparation, Muzamil A Rather, Writing-original draft preparation, Reviewing and Editing, Bindu Naik, Resources, Writing-original draft preparation, Vijay Kumar, Writing-original draft preparation, Eduardo Espinosa, Hilal Ahmad Makroo, Writing-original draft preparation, Reviewing and Editing, Esther Rincón, Writing-original draft preparation, Ramón Morcillo-Martín, Writing-original draft preparation, Hong-Wei Xiao, Reviewing and Editing.
